evidence: Handler methods use precise, accurate names: handleConcordanceExamples, handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison — each name correctly narrows scope
CollocateQueryHelper internal pipeline steps are named precisely: collectHits (phase 1), scoreHits (phase 2), buildAndRankCollocates — each name matches its single responsibility
Domain vocabulary is applied consistently across layers: seed, collocate, lemma, logDice, headword, minLogDice all appear in method signatures, field names, and JSON keys without synonyms

CollocateProfileComparator evokes Java's Comparator<T> contract (compare two things, return int) but the class builds adjective profiles and computes sharing statistics — name/behavior mismatch for anyone familiar with java.util.Comparator

MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection returns an ExplorationResult that contains discovered nouns, core collocates, per-seed maps, and score aggregations — the name focuses on only one sub-step of a much richer operation

SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint and CollocateProfileComparator.deriveAdjectivePattern perform the same conceptual operation (resolve a CQL lookup pattern from grammar config) but use different vocabulary: 'Constraint' vs 'Pattern'

AdjectiveProfile record lives in model/exploration and is referenced even in noun-centric flows; the adjective-specific name creates slight vocabulary friction when reading code paths that involve nouns as collocates
ExploreResponseAssembler, GrammarConfigLoader, GrammarConfigSerializer, RelationPatternBuilder — all four 'doer' class names precisely describe a single, concrete action and do not use generic verbs

strengths: HTTP handler method names are precise and non-redundant: handleConcordanceExamples, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleSemanticFieldComparison each unambiguously identify both the endpoint family and the operation

Internal pipeline steps in CollocateQueryHelper (collectHits, scoreHits, buildAndRankCollocates) use verbs that match the action and nouns that match the data — a reader can reconstruct the algorithm from method names alone
Domain vocabulary is applied with high consistency across all layers: seed/collocate/lemma/logDice never acquire synonyms in parameter names, field names, or JSON keys

'Doer' class names (GrammarConfigLoader, GrammarConfigSerializer, RelationPatternBuilder, ExploreResponseAssembler) all name a single concrete responsibility without generic verbs like Manager or Helper

Record types in the model package (CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, AdjectiveProfile, FetchExamplesOptions, ExplorationOptions) carry specific, domain-grounded names that communicate their role without needing to read their fields
naming_quality.issue_character: The issues are isolated vocabulary choices — one misleading class name (CollocateProfileComparator), one underselling method name (findCollocateIntersection), and one terminology inconsistency ('Constraint' vs 'Pattern') — rather than a systemic pattern of generic or misleading naming.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase exhibits strong naming discipline: domain vocabulary is consistent, handler and pipeline step names are precise, and class names clearly communicate single responsibilities. The handful of issues (CollocateProfileComparator evoking the wrong Java contract, findCollocateIntersection understating its scope, and the Constraint/Pattern terminology split) are real but do not impede comprehension for a developer reading the system as a whole. This places the codebase above the 'significant drag' threshold (60) and closer to 'solid but uneven' (80), landing at 79 because the misleading Comparator name introduces a genuine false expectation that requires reading the class body to resolve.

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator evokes Java's Comparator contract but builds and analyzes profiles, not compares two values
evidence: Class is named CollocateProfileComparator but does not implement java.util.Comparator

Its public method compareCollocateProfiles(Set<String>, double, int) builds AdjectiveProfile lists and computes commonality/distinctiveness scores — a profile-building/analysis operation, not a comparison returning int
Any developer familiar with Java's Comparator contract will expect sort-order semantics and be surprised by the actual API
issues.suggestion: Rename to CollocateProfileAnalyzer or CollocateProfileBuilder to signal that the class computes and aggregates profiles rather than imposing an ordering

issues.summary: MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection names only one sub-step of a method that builds a full ExplorationResult
evidence: Method signature: ExplorationResult findCollocateIntersection(Set<String> seeds, RelationConfig, double, int, int)
Body fetches per-seed collocates, identifies common collocates, builds DiscoveredNoun list, builds CoreCollocate list, assembles per-seed score maps, and constructs the full ExplorationResult
The name 'findCollocateIntersection' describes only the identifyCommonCollocates step and implies the return value is a set or a simple intersection, not a rich exploration result
issues.suggestion: Rename to exploreMultiSeed or buildMultiSeedExplorationResult to reflect that the method performs the full multi-seed exploration pipeline and returns a complete result object

issues.summary: deriveNounCqlConstraint and deriveAdjectivePattern use different vocabulary for the same conceptual operation
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.deriveNounCqlConstraint resolves a CQL lookup string from grammar config using RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern
CollocateProfileComparator.deriveAdjectivePattern does the same thing — calls RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern with a different PosGroup
Both return a CQL pattern string, yet one is called 'Constraint' and the other 'Pattern', introducing unnecessary synonym divergence
issues.suggestion: Standardize both on 'pattern': rename deriveNounCqlConstraint to deriveNounCqlPattern to match the vocabulary used throughout the rest of the codebase (pattern, bcqlPattern, cqlPattern)

root_causes: The Comparator naming mismatch is likely a historical artifact: the class may have started as a simpler pair-comparison utility and grown into a full profile-building component without a name update

The findCollocateIntersection name was probably chosen when the method was first introduced to capture the core algorithmic idea (intersection), and the surrounding scaffolding grew around it without triggering a rename
